section_A.latex_formula: $$ (a, a) \in R \text{ for every } a \in A $$
special_cases: $$ I_A \subseteq R \text{ (Identity relation is a subset)} $$

section_B.when_to_use: To check if every individual element in a given set is related to itself.
common_mistakes: Checking only for some elements of set A instead of ALL elements.
Confusing the identity relation (ONLY (a,a) allowed) with a reflexive relation ((a,a) required, others allowed).
board_traps: Providing a set A = {1, 2, 3} and relation R = {(1,1), (2,2), (1,3)} and asking if it is reflexive. It is NOT, because (3,3) is missing.
section_B.examiner_twist_pattern: Defining a relation mathematically (e.g., |x-y| is even) and asking students to prove reflexivity by substituting y with x.

section_C.why_students_fail: Failing to check the condition against the universal quantifier (for ALL elements 'a' belonging to set A).
section_C.how_to_think: Every element needs a mirror image. If the set is {1,2,3}, I absolutely need (1,1), (2,2), and (3,3) in the relation. If even one is missing, reflexivity breaks.
section_C.thirty_second_strategy: Identify the base set A. Scan the relation R to ensure every element 'x' from A has an (x,x) pair in R.
section_C.mental_shortcut: Reflexive = Mirrors for everyone in the set.

section_A.latex_formula: $$ (a, b) \in R \implies (b, a) \in R \text{ for all } a, b \in A $$
special_cases: $$ \text{Empty relation on a non-empty set is symmetric (vacuously).} $$

section_B.when_to_use: To prove or disprove that a relation works in both directions.
common_mistakes: Assuming that if an element 'a' exists in set A but (a,b) is not in R, symmetry fails. Symmetry only checks pairs ALREADY existing in R.
board_traps: Giving relations like 'x is a brother of y'. If x is y's brother, y could be a sister, making it not symmetric.
section_B.examiner_twist_pattern: Using inequality relations (like a <= b) where symmetry mathematically fails to test counter-example generation.
section_B.why_students_fail: Trying to prove symmetry for elements not present in the relation. It is an 'if-then' implication.
section_B.how_to_think: For every ordered pair I see in R, does its exact reverse also exist in R? If I see (1,2), I must find (2,1).
section_B.thirty_second_strategy: Scan the given relation pairs one by one. Swap the elements of each pair. If the swapped pair is also in the list, it is symmetric.
section_B.micro_example: If R contains (1,2), it MUST contain (2,1). If R contains no distinct pairs, it's still symmetric.

section_A.latex_formula: $$ (a, b) \in R \text{ and } (b, c) \in R \implies (a, c) \in R \text{ for all } a, b, c \in A $$
special_cases: $$ \text{If } (a,b) \in R \text{ but no } (b,c) \in R \text{ exists, } R \text{ is vacuously transitive.} $$

section_B.when_to_use: Testing if a relational property transfers through an intermediary element.
common_mistakes: Declaring a relation non-transitive when the 'if' condition (a,b) and (b,c) isn't even met. It is vacuously transitive in that case.
board_traps: Giving R={(1,2)}. Students say not transitive, but it IS transitive vacuously because there is no (2,c) to trigger the rule.
section_B.examiner_twist_pattern: Testing geometric relations like 'L1 is perpendicular to L2' (not transitive) vs 'L1 is parallel to L2' (transitive).
section_B.why_students_fail: Misunderstanding vacuous truth. The rule is only broken if you have the first two links of the chain but missing the final shortcut.
section_B.how_to_think: Look for a domino effect. If element A connects to B, and B connects to C, does A connect directly to C?
section_B.thirty_second_strategy: Find all pairs (a,b) and (b,c). If you find a chain, look for (a,c). If (a,c) is missing, NOT transitive. If no chains exist at all, it IS transitive.
section_B.micro_example: R={(1,2), (2,3), (1,3)} is transitive. R={(1,2), (2,3)} is not, because (1,3) is missing.

section_A.latex_formula: $$ R \text{ is an equivalence relation } \iff R \text{ is Reflexive, Symmetric, and Transitive} $$
special_cases: $$ \text{The Universal relation on a set is an equivalence relation.} $$
$$ \text{The Identity relation on a set is an equivalence relation.} $$

section_B.when_to_use: When asked to prove equivalence or partition a set into equivalence classes.
common_mistakes: Proving only two of the three properties and concluding it is an equivalence relation.
Writing the equivalence class as a single element instead of a set.
board_traps: Asking for the equivalence class of an element, but students waste time proving equivalence instead of directly finding the class.
section_B.examiner_twist_pattern: Connecting equivalence classes to set partitions (mutually disjoint subsets whose union is the original set).
section_B.why_students_fail: Weakness in algebraic manipulation when proving transitivity for relations like 'a-b is a multiple of n'.
section_B.how_to_think: Equivalence relations group similar items together into distinct, non-overlapping bins. An equivalence class is just the roster of one of those bins.
section_B.thirty_second_strategy: Check R, S, and T systematically. If one fails, stop immediately. For class [a], find all 'x' that satisfy the relation with 'a'.
section_B.micro_example: For relation a=b, [1] = {1}. For a-b is even, [1] = {1, 3, 5...} (all odd numbers).

section_A.latex_formula: $$ f(x_1) = f(x_2) \implies x_1 = x_2 \text{ for all } x_1, x_2 \in \text{Domain} $$
special_cases: $$ f'(x) > 0 \text{ or } f'(x) < 0 \text{ strictly on the domain implies one-one.} $$

section_B.when_to_use: To mathematically prove that a function maps distinct inputs to distinct outputs.
common_mistakes: Ignoring the domain. E.g., f(x)=x^2 is one-one on Natural numbers, but NOT on Integers.
board_traps: Giving a trigonometric function or quadratic and testing if the student notices the restricted domain that makes it one-one.
section_B.examiner_twist_pattern: Using modulus functions |x| or greatest integer functions [x] to test mapping visualization.
section_B.why_students_fail: Testing specific numbers like f(1) and f(-1) without verifying if -1 is actually part of the given domain.
section_B.how_to_think: Every element in the domain must have its own private output. No two inputs can share an output. If they share, it's many-one.
section_B.thirty_second_strategy: Set f(x1) = f(x2). Algebraically solve for x1. If the ONLY valid solution within the domain is x1 = x2, it is one-one.
section_B.mental_shortcut: Horizontal Line Test: If any horizontal line intersects the graph more than once, it is NOT one-one.
section_B.micro_example: f(x) = 2x+3. 2x1+3 = 2x2+3 => 2x1 = 2x2 => x1=x2. Therefore, it is one-one.

section_A.latex_formula: $$\forall y \in \text{Codomain}, \exists x \in \text{Domain} \text{ such that } f(x) = y$$
special_cases: $$\text{If Domain and Codomain are finite and } n(Domain) < n(Codomain)\text{, it can NEVER be onto.}$$

section_B.when_to_use: To prove that every element in the target set (Codomain) is hit by at least one input.
common_mistakes: Finding the range but forgetting to explicitly state that Range = Codomain.
Assuming a function is onto just because the algebraic expression for 'x' in terms of 'y' can be found, without checking if 'x' actually belongs to the Domain.
board_traps: Giving $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined by $f(x) = x^2$. Students solve $x = \pm\sqrt{y}$ and claim it's onto, ignoring that negative $y$ values have no real pre-image.
section_B.examiner_twist_pattern: Restricting the codomain to deliberately make a non-onto function into an onto function (e.g., changing $\mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ to $\mathbb{R} \rightarrow [0, \infty)$).

section_C.why_students_fail: Failure to check the boundaries of the Domain and Codomain given in the question header.
section_C.how_to_think: Think of the Codomain as a group of people and the Domain as a bunch of letters. For the function to be onto, every single person MUST receive at least one letter.
section_C.thirty_second_strategy: 1. Let $f(x) = y$. 2. Solve for $x$ in terms of $y$. 3. Ask yourself: 'For EVERY valid $y$ in the Codomain, does this formula give me an $x$ that legally lives in the Domain?'
section_C.mental_shortcut: Onto = No empty seats in the Codomain.
section_C.micro_example: $f: \mathbb{N} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ where $f(x) = 2x$. Codomain is all natural numbers, but Range is only even numbers. Range $\neq$ Codomain. Not onto.

section_A.latex_formula: $$f \text{ is bijective} \iff f \text{ is injective (one-one) and surjective (onto)}$$
derived_forms: $$n(A) = n(B) \text{ is a necessary condition for finite sets to have a bijection.}$$
special_cases: $$\text{All linear functions } f(x) = ax + b \text{ (where } a \neq 0\text{) from } \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R} \text{ are bijective.}$$

section_B.when_to_use: When asked to show a function establishes a perfect pairing between two sets.
common_mistakes: Proving one-one perfectly but writing 'similarly it is onto' without providing mathematical justification.
board_traps: Giving rational functions like $f(x) = \frac{x-2}{x-3}$. The trap is forgetting to account for the excluded points in the domain and codomain during the onto proof.
section_B.examiner_twist_pattern: Asking to check bijectivity for piecewise functions or functions like $f(n) = n/2$ (if n is even) and $(n+1)/2$ (if n is odd).
section_B.why_students_fail: Lack of structured presentation. Board examiners look for two distinct headings: 'For One-One' and 'For Onto'.
section_B.how_to_think: It's a perfect dance. Every person in Room A has exactly one unique partner in Room B, and nobody in Room B is left sitting alone.
section_B.thirty_second_strategy: Split the page. Left side: Prove $f(x_1) = f(x_2) \implies x_1 = x_2$. Right side: Express $x$ as $g(y)$ and show $g(y) \in$ Domain for all $y \in$ Codomain.
section_B.mental_shortcut: Bijective = Unique outputs AND full coverage.
section_B.micro_example: $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, $f(x) = 2x+1$. One-one because $2x_1+1=2x_2+1 \implies x_1=x_2$. Onto because $x = \frac{y-1}{2}$, which is always real.

derived_forms: $$\text{Total Relations on set A (from A to A)} = 2^{n^2}$$

section_B.when_to_use: MCQ questions asking for the count of possible relations between two finite sets.
trigger_keywords: total subsets of A x B
common_mistakes: Calculating $m \times n$ and stopping, forgetting to raise $2$ to that power.
Confusing 'Number of Relations' ($2^{mn}$) with 'Number of Functions' ($n^m$).
board_traps: Asking for 'number of non-empty relations' and students forgetting to subtract 1 for the null relation $\phi$.
section_B.examiner_twist_pattern: Giving elements indirectly, e.g., 'Set A is the roots of $x^2-5x+6=0$' to force students to find $m$ first.
section_B.why_students_fail: Forgetting that a relation is fundamentally defined as a subset of the Cartesian product $A \times B$.
section_B.how_to_think: First, find how many pairs can be formed ($m \times n$). Every pair acts like a light switch (inclusion/exclusion in a subset). So, 2 choices for each pair.
section_B.thirty_second_strategy: Count elements in set A ($m$). Count elements in set B ($n$). Multiply them ($mn$). The answer is $2^{mn}$.
section_B.micro_example: If $n(A) = 3$ and $n(B) = 2$, total pairs = 6. Total relations = $2^6 = 64$.

special_cases: $$\text{Total functions from A to A} = n^n$$

section_B.when_to_use: When asked how many distinct mappings are possible from set A to set B.
common_mistakes: Flipping the base and exponent (calculating $m^n$ instead of $n^m$). Domain size is the exponent.
board_traps: Giving $n(A) = 0$ (empty set). Number of functions is $n^0 = 1$ (the empty function), which confuses students.
section_B.examiner_twist_pattern: Combining with permutations, or asking for the probability that a randomly formed relation is a function.
section_B.why_students_fail: Memorizing the formula blindly without understanding the fundamental principle of counting.
section_B.how_to_think: Every single element in the Domain ($m$ elements) gets to choose EXACTLY one target from the Codomain ($n$ elements). Element 1 has $n$ choices, AND Element 2 has $n$ choices...
section_B.thirty_second_strategy: Identify Domain size ($m$). Identify Codomain size ($n$). Formula is $(\text{Codomain Size})^{(\text{Domain Size})}$.
section_B.micro_example: $A = \{1, 2, 3\}$, $B = \{a, b\}$. Target size=2, Source size=3. Total functions = $2^3 = 8$.

formulas.formula_title: Total Number of One-One and Bijective Functions

derived_forms: $$\text{Bijective Functions} = \begin{cases} n! & \text{if } m = n \\ 0 & \text{if } m \neq n \end{cases}$$

section_B.when_to_use: To find the count of injective mappings or bijections between finite sets.
common_mistakes: Applying $^nP_m$ even when $m > n$ (which mathematically yields 0, but students try to force a calculation).
board_traps: Asking for number of bijections when $n(A) \neq n(B)$. The answer is instantly 0, but students waste time calculating.
section_B.examiner_twist_pattern: Testing number of onto functions (surjections), which requires the more complex Principle of Inclusion-Exclusion formula.
section_B.why_students_fail: Weak foundation in Permutations from Class 11.
section_B.how_to_think: For one-one: Element 1 has $n$ choices. Element 2 cannot pick what Element 1 picked, so it has $(n-1)$ choices. This is the definition of permutations.
section_B.thirty_second_strategy: Check sizes. If Domain is bigger than Codomain, one-one functions = 0. If equal, bijections = $n!$. If Codomain is bigger, use $^nP_m$.
section_B.micro_example: From $A=\{1,2\}$ to $B=\{a,b,c\}$. $m=2, n=3$. One-one functions = $^3P_2 = 6$. Bijections = 0.

special_cases: $$f \circ I_X = f \text{ and } I_Y \circ f = f$$

section_B.when_to_use: When passing the output of one function directly as the input into another function.
common_mistakes: Assuming $g \circ f = f \circ g$. Composition is generally NOT commutative.
board_traps: Asking for the domain of $gof$. Students only check the domain of $g$, forgetting that $x$ must first be valid for $f$, and $f(x)$ must be valid for $g$.
section_B.examiner_twist_pattern: Giving $f(x)$ and asking to find $f(f(x))$ recursively until a pattern emerges (like $f(f(f(x))) = x$).

section_C.why_students_fail: Losing track of the inner vs. outer function during algebraic substitution.
section_C.how_to_think: Think of it as a factory assembly line. Raw material $x$ goes into Machine F. The partially finished product $f(x)$ then goes into Machine G to get the final product $g(f(x))$.
section_C.thirty_second_strategy: Write the outer function with large empty parentheses wherever 'x' used to be. Then, copy and paste the entire inner function's expression into those empty parentheses.

section_A.latex_formula: $$f \text{ is invertible} \iff f \text{ is bijective (One-One and Onto)}$$
derived_forms: $$f \circ f^{-1} = I_Y \text{ and } f^{-1} \circ f = I_X$$

section_B.when_to_use: When asked to prove a function is invertible or to find its inverse.
common_mistakes: Finding the inverse algebraically without first proving the function is bijective (loses 2 marks).
Leaving the final inverse function in terms of $y$ instead of swapping back to $x$ at the end.
board_traps: Giving quadratic functions restricted to a specific domain (like $[0, \infty)$). Students forget to reject the negative root when solving for $x$ in terms of $y$.
section_B.examiner_twist_pattern: Asking to find the inverse of a rational function like $f(x) = \frac{4x+3}{6x-4}$ and showing that $f = f^{-1}$.

section_C.why_students_fail: Struggling with the algebraic manipulation required to isolate $x$ when $y = f(x)$ is a complex fraction or quadratic.
section_C.how_to_think: An inverse function is just hitting the 'undo' button. If $f$ turns 2 into 10, $f^{-1}$ must turn 10 back into 2. To have a perfect undo button, the original mapping must be one-to-one and onto.
section_C.thirty_second_strategy: 1. Prove one-one. 2. Prove onto. 3. Set $f(x) = y$. 4. Solve purely for $x$. 5. Replace $x$ with $f^{-1}(x)$ and $y$ with $x$.
section_C.mental_shortcut: Inverse = Swap $x$ and $y$, then solve for $y$.

special_cases: $$\text{If } f \text{ and } g \text{ are invertible, then } g \circ f \text{ is also invertible.}$$

section_B.when_to_use: When proving properties of composite inverses or simplifying expressions involving the inverse of a composite function.
common_mistakes: Distributing the inverse sign without swapping the order: incorrectly writing $(gof)^{-1} = g^{-1} o f^{-1}$.
board_traps: Asking to verify the theorem for two given functions. Students calculate the LHS correctly but mess up the composition of the inverses on the RHS.
section_B.examiner_twist_pattern: Giving $f$ and $g$ via sets of ordered pairs instead of algebraic formulas, forcing students to manually trace the reverse mappings.

section_C.why_students_fail: Forgetting the order swap, similar to the transpose property of matrices $(AB)^T = B^T A^T$.
section_C.how_to_think: If you put on your socks (f) and then your shoes (g), to reverse the process, you must FIRST take off your shoes ($g^{-1}$) and THEN take off your socks ($f^{-1}$).
section_C.thirty_second_strategy: To find the inverse of a chain of functions, invert each individual function and completely reverse their sequence.
section_C.mental_shortcut: Socks and Shoes rule: Reverse the order when inverting.

dominant_concepts_from_pyqs: Injectivity and Surjectivity of Piecewise Functions
question_patterns_seen: Long answer questions proving a relation is an equivalence relation and finding elements related to a specific element.
2-mark or 3-mark questions checking if a function is bijective.
MCQs based on identifying the type of relation from a roster form set.
Counter-example based proofs for relations that are not reflexive, symmetric, or transitive.
2-mark questions involving Greatest Integer Function or Modulus function.
3-mark questions asking to check the bijectivity of conditional/piecewise functions defined on Natural numbers.
3-mark counter-example proofs for relations involving powers (e.g., cubes or squares).

high_leverage_ncert_questions.question_text: Show that the relation $$ R $$ in the set $$ A = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} $$ given by $$ R = \{(a, b) : |a - b| \text{ is even}\} $$ is an equivalence relation. Show that all the elements of $$ \{1, 3, 5\} $$ are related to each other and all the elements of $$ \{2, 4\} $$ are related to each other. But no element of $$ \{1, 3, 5\} $$ is related to any element of $$ \{2, 4\} $$.

topper_solution.step_1: Reflexive: Let $$ a \in A $$. $$ |a - a| = 0 $$, which is even. So, $$ (a, a) \in R $$. Therefore, $$ R $$ is reflexive.
topper_solution.step_2: Symmetric: Let $$ (a, b) \in R \implies |a - b| \text{ is even} \implies |-(b - a)| \text{ is even} \implies |b - a| \text{ is even} \implies (b, a) \in R $$. Therefore, $$ R $$ is symmetric.
topper_solution.step_3: Transitive: Let $$ (a, b) \in R $$ and $$ (b, c) \in R $$. This means $$ a - b $$ is even and $$ b - c $$ is even. Summing them: $$ (a - b) + (b - c) = a - c $$ is even. Thus, $$ |a - c| $$ is even $$ \implies (a, c) \in R $$. Since $$ R $$ is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive, it is an equivalence relation.
topper_solution.step_4: For subsets: The difference between any two odd numbers is even, so all elements of $$ \{1, 3, 5\} $$ are related. Similarly, the difference between any two even numbers is even, so $$ \{2, 4\} $$ are related. An odd minus an even is odd, hence elements of $$ \{1, 3, 5\} $$ are not related to $$ \{2, 4\} $$.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.board_writing_tip: Always declare the arbitrary elements you are using, e.g., 'Let $$ a, b, c \in A $$'. Break down the transitivity step by removing the absolute value to show the sum is even.
common_student_mistakes: Failing to properly justify the transition from $$ |a-b| $$ and $$ |b-c| $$ to $$ |a-c| $$ being even.
Skipping the second part of the question regarding equivalence classes.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.question_text: Show that each of the relation $$ R $$ in the set $$ A = \{x \in \mathbb{Z} : 0 \leq x \leq 12\} $$, given by $$ R = \{(a, b) : |a - b| \text{ is a multiple of } 4\} $$ is an equivalence relation. Find the set of all elements related to 1.

topper_solution.step_1: Reflexive: For any $$ a \in A $$, $$ |a - a| = 0 $$, which is a multiple of 4 ($$ 0 = 4 \times 0 $$). Hence, $$ (a, a) \in R $$.
topper_solution.step_2: Symmetric: Let $$ (a, b) \in R $$. Then $$ |a - b| = 4k $$ for some integer $$ k $$. This implies $$ |b - a| = 4k $$, so $$ (b, a) \in R $$.
topper_solution.step_3: Transitive: Let $$ (a, b) \in R $$ and $$ (b, c) \in R $$. Then $$ a - b = \pm 4k_1 $$ and $$ b - c = \pm 4k_2 $$. Adding them gives $$ a - c = \pm 4(k_1 \pm k_2) $$, which is a multiple of 4. So $$ (a, c) \in R $$. Thus, $$ R $$ is an equivalence relation.
topper_solution.step_4: Elements related to 1 are those $$ x \in A $$ such that $$ |x - 1| $$ is a multiple of 4. These are $$ x = 1, 5, 9 $$. So the required set is $$ \{1, 5, 9\} $$.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.board_writing_tip: Explicitly state the integer multiplier $$ k $$ when writing 'multiple of 4' to make the proof mathematically rigorous.
common_student_mistakes: Assuming that $$ 0 $$ is not a multiple of 4, thus failing to prove reflexivity.
Forgetting that the set $$ A $$ restricts the equivalence class to elements within $$ 0 \leq x \leq 12 $$.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.question_text: Show that the relation $$ R $$ in the set $$ \mathbb{R} $$ of real numbers, defined as $$ R = \{(a, b) : a \leq b^2\} $$ is neither reflexive nor symmetric nor transitive.

topper_solution.step_1: Not Reflexive: Consider $$ a = \frac{1}{2} $$. Since $$ \frac{1}{2} \not\leq \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{4} $$, $$ \left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right) \notin R $$.
topper_solution.step_2: Not Symmetric: Consider $$ a = 1, b = 4 $$. Here $$ 1 \leq 4^2 $$ (16), so $$ (1, 4) \in R $$. But $$ 4 \not\leq 1^2 $$ (1), so $$ (4, 1) \notin R $$.
topper_solution.step_3: Not Transitive: Consider $$ a = 3, b = 2, c = 1.5 $$. Here $$ 3 \leq 2^2 $$ (4) and $$ 2 \leq 1.5^2 $$ (2.25), so $$ (3, 2) \in R $$ and $$ (2, 1.5) \in R $$. However, $$ 3 \not\leq 1.5^2 $$ (2.25), so $$ (3, 1.5) \notin R $$.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.board_writing_tip: When proving a relation is 'neither', always use specific numerical counter-examples. Do not attempt to prove it algebraically with variables.
common_student_mistakes: Trying to prove the negative using general variables $$ a, b, c $$ instead of providing a counter-example.
Using integers instead of fractions to check reflexivity, incorrectly concluding it is reflexive because $$ 2 \leq 2^2 $$.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.question_text: Let $$ A = \mathbb{R} - \{3\} $$ and $$ B = \mathbb{R} - \{1\} $$. Consider the function $$ f : A \rightarrow B $$ defined by $$ f(x) = \frac{x - 2}{x - 3} $$. Is $$ f $$ one-one and onto? Justify your answer.

topper_solution.step_1: One-one check: Let $$ f(x_1) = f(x_2) $$. Then $$ \frac{x_1 - 2}{x_1 - 3} = \frac{x_2 - 2}{x_2 - 3} $$. Cross multiplying: $$ (x_1 - 2)(x_2 - 3) = (x_2 - 2)(x_1 - 3) $$.
topper_solution.step_2: $$ x_1 x_2 - 3x_1 - 2x_2 + 6 = x_1 x_2 - 3x_2 - 2x_1 + 6 $$. Simplifying gives $$ -x_1 = -x_2 \implies x_1 = x_2 $$. Hence, $$ f $$ is one-one.
topper_solution.step_3: Onto check: Let $$ y \in B $$, so $$ y \neq 1 $$. Let $$ y = \frac{x - 2}{x - 3} $$. Expressing $$ x $$ in terms of $$ y $$: $$ y(x - 3) = x - 2 \implies xy - 3y = x - 2 \implies x(y - 1) = 3y - 2 \implies x = \frac{3y - 2}{y - 1} $$.
topper_solution.step_4: Since $$ y \neq 1 $$, $$ x $$ is a well-defined real number. Also, to check if $$ x \in A $$, if $$ x = 3 \implies \frac{3y - 2}{y - 1} = 3 \implies 3y - 2 = 3y - 3 \implies -2 = -3 $$, which is impossible. Thus $$ x \in A $$. Since for every $$ y \in B $$, there exists $$ x \in A $$ such that $$ f(x) = y $$, $$ f $$ is onto.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.board_writing_tip: For onto proofs involving rational functions, explicitly show that the calculated $$ x $$ belongs to the domain by proving the denominator does not make it undefined and $$ x $$ does not equal the excluded value of the domain.
common_student_mistakes: Forgetting to verify that $$ x $$ calculated for onto proof actually belongs to the domain $$ A $$.
Making algebraic sign errors during cross-multiplication for injectivity.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.question_text: In each of the following cases, state whether the function is one-one, onto or bijective. Justify your answer. (i) $$ f : \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R} $$ defined by $$ f(x) = 3 - 4x $$ (ii) $$ f : \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R} $$ defined by $$ f(x) = 1 + x^2 $$

topper_solution.step_1: (i) For one-one: Let $$ f(x_1) = f(x_2) $$. Then $$ 3 - 4x_1 = 3 - 4x_2 \implies -4x_1 = -4x_2 \implies x_1 = x_2 $$. Thus, one-one. For onto: Let $$ y \in \mathbb{R} $$. Let $$ y = 3 - 4x \implies 4x = 3 - y \implies x = \frac{3 - y}{4} $$. Since $$ \frac{3 - y}{4} \in \mathbb{R} $$ for all $$ y \in \mathbb{R} $$, it is onto. So, bijective.
topper_solution.step_2: (ii) For one-one: $$ f(1) = 1 + 1^2 = 2 $$ and $$ f(-1) = 1 + (-1)^2 = 2 $$. Since $$ f(1) = f(-1) $$ but $$ 1 \neq -1 $$, it is not one-one.
topper_solution.step_3: (ii) For onto: The range of $$ 1 + x^2 $$ is $$ [1, \infty) $$. Let $$ y = -2 \in \mathbb{R} $$ (codomain). There is no real $$ x $$ such that $$ 1 + x^2 = -2 \implies x^2 = -3 $$. Thus, not onto. Hence, neither one-one nor onto.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.board_writing_tip: If a function is not one-one or not onto, providing a single clear counter-example is sufficient and preferred over general proofs.
common_student_mistakes: Trying to prove $$ f(x) = 1 + x^2 $$ is not one-one algebraically by writing $$ x_1^2 = x_2^2 \implies x_1 = \pm x_2 $$ without concluding properly.
Confusing the domain and codomain sets (e.g., if it were $$ \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N} $$, part (i) would not be onto).

high_leverage_ncert_questions.question_text: Let $$ R $$ be the relation in the set $$ \{1, 2, 3, 4\} $$ given by $$ R = \{(1, 2), (2, 2), (1, 1), (4, 4), (1, 3), (3, 3), (3, 2)\} $$. Choose the correct answer regarding its reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity.
concepts_tested: Identifying Reflexive, Symmetric, Transitive Relations from Roster Form

topper_solution.step_1: Check reflexivity: The set is $$ \{1, 2, 3, 4\} $$. $$ (1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3), (4, 4) $$ are all present in $$ R $$. So, $$ R $$ is reflexive.
topper_solution.step_2: Check symmetry: $$ (1, 2) \in R $$, but $$ (2, 1) \notin R $$. So, $$ R $$ is not symmetric.
topper_solution.step_3: Check transitivity: We check pairs. $$ (1, 3) \in R $$ and $$ (3, 2) \in R $$. For transitivity, $$ (1, 2) $$ must be in $$ R $$, which is true. Checking all such valid pairs confirms $$ R $$ is transitive.
topper_solution.step_4: Conclusion: $$ R $$ is reflexive and transitive but not symmetric.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.board_writing_tip: For MCQs, mentally verify each condition or scribble short counter-examples in rough work. Don't waste time writing full theoretical proofs.
common_student_mistakes: Incorrectly identifying transitivity because a pair like $$ (3, 2) $$ does not have a subsequent pair starting with 2 (except $$ (2, 2) $$) to violate the condition, forgetting that if $$ (a, b) \in R $$ and no $$ (b, c) \in R $$ exists, transitivity is not violated for that element.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.question_text: Check whether the relation $$ R $$ in $$ \mathbb{R} $$ defined by $$ R = \{(a, b) : a \leq b^3\} $$ is reflexive, symmetric or transitive.
concepts_tested: Counter-examples using fractions and negative numbers

topper_solution.step_1: Reflexivity: Let $$ a = \frac{1}{2} $$. We know that $$ \frac{1}{2} \not\leq \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3 = \frac{1}{8} $$. Therefore, $$ \left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right) \notin R $$. So, $$ R $$ is not reflexive.
topper_solution.step_2:
Symmetry: Let $$ a = 1, b = 2 $$. We have $$ 1 \leq 2^3 $$ (i.e., $$ 1 \leq 8 $$), so $$ (1, 2) \in R $$. But $$ 2 
ot\leq 1^3 $$ (i.e., $$ 2 
ot\leq 1 $$), so $$ (2, 1) 
otin R $$. So, $$ R $$ is not symmetric.
topper_solution.step_3:
Transitivity: Let $$ a = 3, b =  rac{3}{2}, c =  rac{6}{5} $$. Here $$ 3 \leq \left( rac{3}{2}ight)^3 $$ (i.e., $$ 3 \leq 3.375 $$) implies $$ \left(3,  rac{3}{2}ight) \in R $$. Also, $$  rac{3}{2} \leq \left( rac{6}{5}ight)^3 $$ (i.e., $$ 1.5 \leq 1.728 $$) implies $$ \left( rac{3}{2},  rac{6}{5}ight) \in R $$. However, $$ 3 
ot\leq \left( rac{6}{5}ight)^3 $$ (i.e., $$ 3 
ot\leq 1.728 $$), so $$ \left(3,  rac{6}{5}ight) 
otin R $$. So, $$ R $$ is not transitive.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.board_writing_tip: The transitivity counter-example for this specific question is famously difficult to invent on the spot during the exam. Memorize a simple counter-example set like $$ (3, 1.5, 1.2) $$ or $$ (10, 3, 2) $$.
common_student_mistakes: Incorrectly assuming it is transitive because they only test integer values where it often holds true.
Failing to explicitly state the calculated cube values to prove the inequality fails.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.question_text: Let $$ f: \mathbb{N} \rightarrow \mathbb{N} $$ be defined by $$ f(n) = \frac{n+1}{2} $$ if $$ n $$ is odd, and $$ f(n) = \frac{n}{2} $$ if $$ n $$ is even for all $$ n \in \mathbb{N} $$. State whether the function $$ f $$ is bijective. Justify your answer.

topper_solution.step_1: One-one check: Let $$ n_1 = 1 $$ (odd) and $$ n_2 = 2 $$ (even). Then $$ f(1) = \frac{1+1}{2} = 1 $$ and $$ f(2) = \frac{2}{2} = 1 $$. Since $$ f(1) = f(2) $$ but $$ 1 \neq 2 $$, the function $$ f $$ is many-one, not one-one.
topper_solution.step_2: Onto check: Let $$ y \in \mathbb{N} $$ (codomain). We need to find if there exists an $$ n \in \mathbb{N} $$ (domain) such that $$ f(n) = y $$.
topper_solution.step_3: If we take $$ n = 2y $$, which is always an even natural number since $$ y \in \mathbb{N} $$. Then according to the definition, $$ f(n) = f(2y) = \frac{2y}{2} = y $$.
topper_solution.step_4: Since for every $$ y \in \mathbb{N} $$, there exists a pre-image $$ 2y \in \mathbb{N} $$, the function is onto. Conclusion: Since it is not one-one, $$ f $$ is not bijective.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.board_writing_tip: Always check injectivity with small numbers first. If you find a single collision (like 1 and 2), you can immediately declare it not one-one and save time on algebra.
common_student_mistakes: Attempting to prove one-one using general variables $$ n_1, n_2 $$ and getting confused by the odd/even cases.
Declaring it is not onto just because it's not one-one.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.question_text: Prove that the Greatest Integer Function $$ f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R} $$, given by $$ f(x) = [x] $$, is neither one-one nor onto, where $$ [x] $$ denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to $$ x $$.

topper_solution.step_1: Not One-One: Let $$ x_1 = 1.2 $$ and $$ x_2 = 1.8 $$. Both belong to the domain $$ \mathbb{R} $$.
topper_solution.step_3: Not Onto: Let $$ y = 0.5 $$. Here, $$ y \in \mathbb{R} $$ (the codomain).
topper_solution.step_4: By definition, $$ [x] $$ always outputs an integer. There is no real value of $$ x $$ such that $$ [x] = 0.5 $$. Therefore, 0.5 has no pre-image in the domain $$ \mathbb{R} $$. Thus, $$ f $$ is not onto.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.board_writing_tip: Keep the counter-examples simple. Standard non-integer decimal values perfectly demonstrate the many-one and into nature of the GIF.
common_student_mistakes: Overcomplicating the proof by using theoretical definitions of the Greatest Integer Function instead of clear numeric examples.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.question_text: Show that the relation $$ R $$ in the set $$ A $$ of all the books in a library of a college, given by $$ R = \{(x, y) : x \text{ and } y \text{ have same number of pages}\} $$ is an equivalence relation.

topper_solution.step_1: Reflexive: Let $$ x \in A $$. Since a book $$ x $$ has the same number of pages as itself, $$ (x, x) \in R $$. So, $$ R $$ is reflexive.
topper_solution.step_2: Symmetric: Let $$ (x, y) \in R $$. This means book $$ x $$ and book $$ y $$ have the same number of pages. This implies book $$ y $$ and book $$ x $$ have the same number of pages, so $$ (y, x) \in R $$. Thus, $$ R $$ is symmetric.
topper_solution.step_3: Transitive: Let $$ (x, y) \in R $$ and $$ (y, z) \in R $$. This means $$ x $$ and $$ y $$ have the same number of pages, and $$ y $$ and $$ z $$ have the same number of pages. Consequently, $$ x $$ and $$ z $$ must have the same number of pages. So, $$ (x, z) \in R $$. Thus, $$ R $$ is transitive.
topper_solution.step_4: Since $$ R $$ is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive, it is an equivalence relation.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.board_writing_tip: Write the logical statements clearly in words as the relation is defined in words. Do not introduce unnecessary mathematical variables representing the number of pages.
common_student_mistakes: Writing ambiguous statements without defining that x, y, z are 'books in the library A'.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.question_text: Show that the Modulus Function $$ f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R} $$, given by $$ f(x) = |x| $$, is neither one-one nor onto, where $$ |x| $$ is $$ x $$, if $$ x $$ is positive or 0 and $$ |x| $$ is $$ -x $$, if $$ x $$ is negative.

topper_solution.step_1: Not One-One: Let $$ x_1 = 1 $$ and $$ x_2 = -1 $$. Both are in the domain $$ \mathbb{R} $$.
topper_solution.step_2: $$ f(1) = |1| = 1 $$ and $$ f(-1) = |-1| = 1 $$. Because $$ f(1) = f(-1) $$ but $$ 1 \neq -1 $$, the function is not one-one.
topper_solution.step_3: Not Onto: Let $$ y = -2 $$. Here $$ y \in \mathbb{R} $$ (the codomain).
topper_solution.step_4: By definition of the modulus function, $$ f(x) = |x| \geq 0 $$ for all $$ x \in \mathbb{R} $$. There is no real value of $$ x $$ such that $$ |x| = -2 $$. Thus, -2 has no pre-image. Therefore, $$ f $$ is not onto.

high_leverage_ncert_questions.board_writing_tip: Directly state a negative number from the codomain as your counter-example for showing it is not onto. It is the fastest and most mathematically rigorous method.
common_student_mistakes: Only defining the modulus function mathematically without actually solving the question.
Stating 'Range is [0, infinity)' without explicitly showing a negative number failing to have a pre-image.
